accept.reason: scripts/_accept.py is a cohesive fail-closed acceptance-policy module (parse/validate + 3-kind match). After helper extraction its per-function cyclomatic complexity is all <=3; the module-level maintainability_index (~42.7) reflects the aggregate size of one coherent contract, not per-unit complexity. Splitting it to chase MI would fragment a single contract across files without reducing real complexity (cf. the accepted scripts/mprr_normalize.py module-MI residual). Accepted as deferred-structural.

scripts/wave_frozen.md is the acceptance ledger for the repo — it grows with every ratchet/triage session by design. High churn on this file is the expected signal of an active audit cycle, not a code quality risk.

W8202 fires on `_MATCH_KEYS` (a module-level tuple) inside `_parse_fields`. The loop iterates at most 5 elements during policy loading — a cold path called once at startup. Hoisting `_MATCH_KEYS` to a local variable adds indirection with no measurable benefit.

W8205 fires on `os.walk` and `os.path.join` in `scan_repo_profile`. `os.walk(repo_root)` is the iterable being iterated over, not called inside the loop body; perflint incorrectly treats the outer-scope reference as a repeated dotted import. For `os.path.join` the call is one-time per directory entry in a disk-IO-bound scan where the attribute lookup cost is negligible.

W8202 fires on `dir_names`, `file_names` accesses inside `scan_repo_profile`'s `os.walk` loop. These are loop variables unpacked from the `os.walk` iterator — they are not module-level globals. perflint misidentifies them as global lookups because they shadow outer-scope names.

W8201 fires on `manifest["lanes"][lane_name]` inside `_evaluate_active_lanes` and on similar subscript-then-index patterns in `_build_merged_skills`. The outer subscript `manifest["lanes"]` is invariant but the full expression `manifest["lanes"][lane_name]` is loop-variant (key changes per iteration). perflint W8201 over-approximates by flagging the outer dict access rather than the full indexing expression.

W8202 fires on `KNOWN_LANGUAGES`, `KNOWN_TEST_SYSTEMS` (module-level frozensets) inside `_matches_when`. These are lookup-table constants iterated or membership-tested once per condition key in a cold-path lane-selection function called at startup. Hoisting them adds no value.

W8201 fires on `manifest["lanes"][lane_name]` and `manifest["skills"][skill_name]` patterns in `_collect_active_and_strict_skills` and `_mark_blocking_skills`. The outer dict `manifest["lanes"]` is loop-invariant but the full subscript expression changes with each iteration variable. perflint over-approximates — hoisting `manifest["lanes"]` to a local would only avoid one dict lookup per call, not per iteration.

W8301 fires on `warnings_list = []` in `_evaluate_lane_by_type`. This empty list is subsequently concatenated with `eval_warnings` (a list returned by the evaluator) using `+`. Changing to `()` would produce `tuple + list` which raises TypeError at runtime. The list must remain a list to support the concatenation contract.

W8202 fires on `_REQUIRED_SKILL_FIELDS` (module-level frozenset) inside `_validate_skill_entry_fields`. The loop validates skill config fields on startup — a cold path with at most ~10 iterations. Hoisting to a local variable adds noise without measurable gain.

W8205 fires on `json.loads` inside `_load_kpis`'s line-by-line file read loop. Importing `loads` directly before the loop is a micro-optimization; this is an IO-bound analytics script called once per run.

W8201 fires on `[repo for repo in active_repos if alloc[repo] < cap]` inside the greedy allocator loop. The expression is loop-variant: `alloc[repo]` changes each iteration when `alloc[winner] += 1` is applied. perflint flags `active_repos` and `cap` as invariant components but the full comprehension must be re-evaluated every iteration.

W8205 fires on `importlib.import_module` inside the module re-export loop. Each iteration imports a different module, so the function must be called once per iteration by design. Hoisting with `from importlib import import_module` would not change semantics but the per-call cost is dominated by the Python import machinery, not the attribute lookup.

W8202 fires on `globals()`, `getattr`, `importlib` accesses inside the module re-export loop. These are built-in functions and an already-imported module. The loop runs once at module import time over a handful of entries. No measurable performance impact.

W8301 fires on `cmd = [sys.executable, runner, ...]` in `_run_wave`. The list is immediately extended with `cmd += [...]` multiple times before being passed to `subprocess.run`. Changing to a tuple would make `+=` rebind the name to a new tuple each time; the current pattern with a mutable list is idiomatic and intentional.

W8205 fires on `shutil.copy2` inside `graduate`'s glob loop. Each iteration copies one file; the loop body is dominated by file-copy IO. The cost of a single attribute lookup on `shutil` is unmeasurable relative to disk IO.

W8202 fires on `r.get(...)` calls inside `_build_accept_doc`'s loop. `r` is the loop variable (a dict from the baseline rows), not a global. perflint misidentifies it as a global lookup because `r` is not declared in the loop header as a classical iteration variable name.

W8205 fires on `json.loads` inside a line-by-line file-reading loop in `_parse_worker_events`. The loop is IO-bound (file reading); the attribute lookup on `json` is negligible. This is a cold-path analytics script called at most once per CI run.

W8201 fires on `ev.get("event")` inside a line-parsing loop. `ev` changes per iteration (it is unpacked from `json.loads(line)`), so the expression is loop-variant. perflint over-approximates: it sees the `.get` call as potentially invariant because the method binding is stable, but the object `ev` itself is not.

W8301 fires on `worker_runs = []` in the except branch of `_derive_worker_runs` fallback. `worker_runs` is passed to `KpiInputs(worker_runs=...)` whose field is typed as `list[dict]`. Changing to `()` would violate the type contract and fail any downstream append or len operation.

W8202 fires on `_REDUNDANCY_LEAVES` and `_CLASS_BY_LEAF` (module-level frozen lookup tables) inside `normalize`'s finding loop. These are constant dicts consulted once per finding. The loop is not a hot path — it processes at most a few hundred findings per audit run.

W8205 fires on `mprr_packets.remediation_packet` inside a batch-dispatch loop. The attribute lookup traverses one module reference per iteration; the batch size is small (typically 1-5 items). The call itself performs JSON serialization and subprocess coordination — the attribute lookup cost is unmeasurable.

W8201 fires on `list(it.files)` and `set(it.files)` inside the batch-dispatch loop. `it` changes per iteration (each element of `batch`), so both expressions are loop-variant. perflint incorrectly flags the `list()` and `set()` constructors as invariant calls.

W8402 fires on `entries.append(...)` inside a nested loop in `_engine_accept_policy` (outer loop: `data.values()`, inner: `section.get('exclude_paths', [])`). The outer loop filters with `isinstance` check and pulls `reason` from each section; these per-section values are used in the constructed `AcceptEntry`. This is not a simple list copy — it cannot be replaced with `list.copy()` or a single-level comprehension.

W8205 fires on `time.time()` calls inside `_collect_lane_results`'s parallel-future loop. `time` is a C-extension module; the attribute lookup overhead is negligible. The concurrency bookkeeping (`start_times`, `timings`) requires `time.time()` at precise positions in the loop body, and the loop runs once per lane (typically 8-9 iterations).

W8201 fires at multiple sites in `_partition_runnable` and `_run_wave`: on `context.rev is None` (dataclass attribute read), on `results[lane]` dict indexing, and on `_status_for_exit(exit_code, len(findings))`. These are all loop-variant: `lane` changes each iteration, making the dict lookups and function arguments different each time. perflint W8201 over-approximates by treating frozen-dataclass attribute reads and per-key dict accesses as potential loop-invariants.

W8205 fires on `json.dumps` inside `mechanical_patches`'s finding loop. Each call serializes a per-finding `verify_payload` dict to disk; the loop body is dominated by file-write IO. Moving `from json import dumps` before the loop has no measurable effect.

W8202 fires on `SAFE_PATCH_TABLE` (module-level dict constant) inside `mechanical_patches`'s finding loop. This is a dispatch table consulted once per finding to determine the patch strategy. The lookup is O(1) and not on a hot path.

W8201 fires on multiple statements inside `mechanical_patches`'s finding loop: `str(finding.get('id', 'unknown'))`, `str(finding.get('path', ''))`, `entry['check_cmd']`. These all depend on `finding` or `entry` which change each iteration. perflint W8201 over-approximates — the `str()` constructor reference is invariant but its argument is loop-variant.

W8301 fires on `KPIS = [...]` module-level test fixture in test_allocate_batches.py. Test fixtures are conventionally written as lists to allow parametrize/append patterns. Changing to a tuple provides no test-quality benefit and would require updating all usages if the fixture grows.

W8202 fires on module-level name accesses inside test helper loops in test_check_skill_requirements.py. These are test utility functions called rarely in test setup, not hot paths. The suggested optimization would reduce test readability without measurable benefit.

W8201 fires on dict subscript patterns inside test helper loops (e.g., `manifest['skills'][name]`). The loop variable `name` changes each iteration making the full expression loop-variant. perflint over-approximates by flagging the outer subscript as invariant.

W8403 fires on `_lane_manifest`'s `skills` dict building loop at line 1093. The loop body assigns identical value structures for each `name`; a dict comprehension would work, but the current for-loop form makes the value template (`priority`, `source_type`, etc.) more legible in test code where clarity outweighs micro-optimization.

W8301 fires on list literals used as test parametrize values and fixture data in test_check_skill_requirements.py. Test fixtures are idiomatically written as lists; switching to tuples in test code is a style noise with no runtime benefit.
W8301 fires on a list literal used as test parametrize data in test_lessons.py. Test data fixtures are idiomatically lists; no runtime benefit from changing to tuple.
W8301 fires on test fixture list literals in test_migrate_baseline.py. Test fixtures are idiomatically lists; no benefit from changing to tuples in test code.
W8301 fires on test fixture list literals in test_mprr_normalize.py. Test fixtures are idiomatically lists; no runtime benefit from changing to tuples.

W8202 fires on module-level constant accesses inside test assertion loops in test_mprr_partition.py. These are cold-path test functions; performance optimization in test assertion loops is not warranted.
W8301 fires on test fixture list literals in test_mprr_run.py. Test data is idiomatically expressed as lists; no runtime benefit from converting to tuples in test code.

W8301 fires on the `["tests", "fixtures"]` list in `test_effective_excludes_defaults_to_tests_and_fixtures`. This asserts the exact return value of `_effective_excludes`; changing to a tuple would require updating the assertion and the production function's return type. The assertion clarity outweighs the micro-optimization.

W8202 fires on module-level name accesses inside test verification loops in test_self_dogfood.py. These loops run once per test execution over small collections. No hot path, no measurable benefit from hoisting.

W8201 fires on expressions inside test verification loops in test_self_dogfood.py where the expression depends on the loop variable. perflint over-approximates by flagging outer object references as invariant when the full subscripted expression is loop-variant.

W8301 fires on test fixture list literals used as finding collections in test_synthesize_packets.py. Test fixtures are idiomatically lists to allow append/extend during parametrize setup; no runtime benefit from changing to tuples.
W8202 fires on a module-level constant accessed inside a test assertion loop in test_synthesize_perf.py. This is a cold-path test with negligible iteration count. No benefit from hoisting.

W8301 fires on `FINDINGS = [...]` module-level test fixture list in test_wave_findings.py. The fixture is used in multiple test functions as a shared input; list form is idiomatic for test fixtures and allows easier extension.
